headline: E-invoice exemption raised to RM1m — shadow economy is around RM330B
context: On 6 December 2025 PM Anwar raised Malaysia's e-invoice exemption from RM500,000 to RM1m. SAMENTA says it spares 200,000 SMEs. LHDN designed the system to narrow a shadow economy estimated at 21% of GDP — roughly RM330 billion.

cards.big: On 6 December 2025 PM Anwar raised the e-invoice exemption from RM500,000 to RM1m. SAMENTA said 200,000 more SMEs are now exempt.
cards.sub: The e-invoice system was designed to narrow Malaysia's shadow economy — estimated at 21% of GDP, roughly RM330 billion.

cards.big: Malaysia's shadow economy is estimated at 21% of GDP — roughly RM330 billion. Tax-gap losses are cited at over RM70 billion a year.
cards.sub: Estimates range 21-25% across methodologies (UiTM, IJBS, World Economics). The tax gap is the explicit rationale LHDN gave for e-invoicing.

cards.big: LHDN reported 820.5 million e-invoices from 111,600 taxpayers by 22 December 2025 — Phase 1-2 compliance over 90%.
cards.sub: Phase 3 (RM5-25m turnover) reached 80%. Volume more than doubled from 328m in June 2025. The CEO said the mandate is working where enforced.

cards.big: One month later, on 5 January 2026, Anwar postponed e-invoicing for RM1-5m firms by another year.
cards.sub: In November 2025 LHDN alleged RM70m evasion at one luxury-car cartel. In July 2025 it flagged 5,800 individuals for RM484m undeclared income.

cards.big: The question is not whether small firms can afford e-invoicing. It is whose books stay invisible when the rule is relaxed.
E-invoicing's promise was that every transaction leaves a trail. The cost relief is real. So is the policy choice to leave most of the shadow economy unrecorded.
